aliases: South Indian Mutton Curry with Roasted Coconut

english: South Indian Mutton Curry with Roasted Coconut

food_subgroup: Meat-based curry with coconut and spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu. FOODON_00002700 (curry food) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR (meat-based curry dish) is the closest available parent. No specific code exists for varutharaicha kuzhambu in the FoodEx2 catalogue.

preparation_method: slow simmering of mutton in spiced gravy

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of mutton pieces in a thick roasted-coconut gravy, usually accompanied by rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry (Indian-style) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for mutton and coconut components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for mutton curry (Indian-style). Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu is a South Indian variant distinguished by a dry-roasted coconut and spice paste. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry baseline with upward adjustment to fat and energy to account for the roasted coconut component, cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked goat meat (NDB 17168) and dried coconut (NDB 12111). The adaptation is conservative and represents a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat curry reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu. GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate meat curry dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The dish is predominantly protein and fat with a low net carbohydrate content (~4.2 g/100 g), consistent with a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 4.2 × 3) / 100 = 3.78, rounded to 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g; ~960 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full 300 g serving contributes approximately 960 mg sodium, representing roughly 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low net carbohydrate content (~4.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate density, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or refined bread; the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 3.8.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from roasted coconut and mutton (~5.8 g/100 g saturated fat; ~17.4 g per 300 g serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The roasted coconut and mutton fat contribute a moderate saturated fat load (~17.4 g per serving), which exceeds the recommended daily saturated fat intake of 20 g for many adults; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g and choose leaner cuts of mutton to reduce saturated fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu is a traditional South Indian mutton curry originating from Tamil Nadu, India, and is particularly associated with Chettinad and Tamil Brahmin culinary traditions. The defining characteristic of this dish is the freshly dry-roasted coconut and spice paste — 'varutharaicha' literally means 'roasted and ground' in Tamil — which is prepared by dry-roasting grated coconut together with whole spices such as coriander seeds, cumin, black pepper, and dried red chillies until deeply aromatic and golden, then grinding them into a coarse paste. This roasted paste is the flavour backbone of the curry and distinguishes it from coconut-milk-based curries. Mutton pieces are slow-simmered in a base of onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and turmeric until tender, then the roasted coconut-spice paste is incorporated and cooked down to a thick, intensely flavoured gravy. The resulting dish has a rich, nutty, smoky depth from the roasted coconut, a robust spice profile, and a thick, clinging sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (~12.5 g/100 g) and iron (~2.1 mg/100 g) from the mutton, with moderate fat content elevated by the coconut component (~11.5 g/100 g total fat, ~5.8 g saturated). Sodium is moderately high (~320 mg/100 g). The glycemic index is low (~30) due to the minimal carbohydrate content, making it appropriate for glycaemic management when paired with low-GI accompaniments. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, this dish is served in South Indian restaurants and is popular among the Tamil community.
common_user_queries: How is varutharaicha kuzhambu different from regular mutton curry?
Is varutharaicha kari kuzhambu good for diabetics?
What spices are used in varutharaicha kuzhambu?
South Indian roasted coconut mutton curry nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry data with adjustment for the roasted coconut component, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. No exact match for Varutharaicha Kari Kuzhambu exists in any approved primary source database. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-carbohydrate meat curry dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category and is not reliably quantified for Indian meat curry preparations in approved databases. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated or source-derived numeric values where defensible. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~30) based on low-carbohydrate meat curry references from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI Values; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values represent a per-100 g estimate for the prepared curry including gravy and meat in typical proportions. The dish is served at Manam - Upper Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 29.00 per portion. The food_id GD004253 is a field survey-sourced seed record.
